claim: The design criterion for Fe-Mn-Al-C structural steels requires an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) ≥ 1100 MPa and a U-notch impact toughness (K_CU) ≥ 50 J/cm².
evidence_text: From the mechanical properties point of view, the criterion is based on the best combination of mechanical strength (UTS . 1100 MPa) and impact toughness (K_CU > 50 J/cm^2).

claim: The microstructural criterion for an optimum state in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels is the absence of δ-ferrite initially and the prevention of coarse grain-boundary precipitation and β-Mn formation during thermal processing.
evidence_text: From the microstructural point of view, the criterion is based on the absence of d-ferrite in the starting structure, and, in the course of further thermal processing, on the prevention of coarse grain-boundary precipitation and on the prevention of a decomposition reaction that leads to the appearance of b-Mn.

claim: Aging at 550 °C produces considerable hardening when the aluminum content exceeds 7%.
evidence_text: It was established that considerable hardening is obtained by aging at 550 °C when the aluminum content exceeds 7% (Fig. 1).

claim: According to Huang et al., precipitation of κ-phase particles within the matrix at 650 °C occurs when the aluminum content is higher than 6.2%.
evidence_text: According to Huang et al.,[20] precipitation of kphase particles within the matrix at 650 °C occurs when the aluminum content is higher than 6.2%.

claim: With up to 12% δ-ferrite in the cast steel, subsequent hot working within the range 1150 to 1170 °C dissolves the δ-ferrite.
evidence_text: With up to 12% d-ferrite in the cast steel, subsequent hot working within the range 1150 to 1170 °C is accompanied by the d-ferrite dissolution.

claim: The temperature of 1150 °C is recommended for δ-ferrite dissolution.
evidence_text: and so it can be established that the temperature of 1150 °C is the recommended temperature for d-ferrite dissolution.

claim: In electroslag-remelted ingots of 415×415 mm cross section and 2.2 tonnes mass, after rolling to 20 mm diameter rods or 105×105 mm square cross section, δ-ferrite is absent for aluminum contents under 9.7%.
evidence_text: In the central part of ingots with a square cross section (415 × 415 mm) and a mass of 2.2 metric tonnes, which are obtained by electroslag melting, after subsequent rolling (with production of rods 20 mm in diameter or a square cross section up to 105 × 105 mm) has been completed, d-ferrite is absent in the steel structure for aluminum contents under 9.7%.

claim: For aluminum content lower than 9.5%, δ-ferrite is absent in hot-worked semifinished products; however, if the hot-working temperature exceeds 1170 °C, an additional amount of δ-ferrite can form, noting the solidus temperature of Fe-29Mn-9Al-0.9C steel is about 1320 °C.
evidence_text: For aluminum content lower than 9.5%, d-ferrite is absent in hot-worked semifinished products. However, if the hot-working temperature exceeds 1170 °C (the solidus temperature of Fe-29Mn-9Al-0.9C steel is about 1320 °C), an additional amount of d-ferrite can be formed in the structure.

claim: Aging Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel in the temperature range of 400 to 850 °C leads to precipitation of κ-phase particles that exhibit magnetic properties.
evidence_text: Aging this steel within the temperature range 400 to 850 °C allows the precipitation of k-phase particles with magnetic properties.[12]

claim: For a fixed Al content, the saturation magnetization (4πJs) increases with carbon only up to a limit; further increases require a higher Al content, indicating that the extent of aging depends on aluminum concentration.
evidence_text: For the same aluminum content, the value of 4πJs increases only up to a certain carbon concentration. To raise this value, it is necessary to increase the amount of aluminum in steel. Thus, the extent of the aging process depends on the aluminum content.

claim: After aging at 550 °C for 16 h, carbon concentrations above 1 wt% in Fe-Mn-Al-C steels cause grain-boundary carbide precipitation, resulting in a decrease in U-notch impact toughness by at least 40 J/cm².
evidence_text: In the aged (550 °C, 16 h) state, carbon concentrations exceeding 1% lead to the appearance of boundary carbide precipitations, and Unotch impact toughness decreases at least 40 J/cm2 (Fig. 6).

claim: In Fe-Mn-Al-C steels with 0.9% C and 9% Al, manganese below 25 wt% leads to δ-ferrite formation, while manganese exceeding 31–32 wt% causes β-Mn precipitation after prolonged aging at 550 °C, resulting in extreme brittleness.
evidence_text: An Mn concentration less than 25% allows the appearance of d-ferrite, whereas a concentration exceeding 31 to 32% Mn will give origin to b-Mn precipitation after long-time aging at 550 °C, causing extreme brittleness.

claim: Based on second-order polynomial modeling of UTS and K_CU as functions of the steel composition, UTS ≥ 1100 MPa and K_CU ≥ 50 J/cm² can be obtained for carbon content 0.84–0.99%, aluminum 8.8–9.7%, and manganese 28.5–30.5%.
evidence_text: The analysis of variations of mechanical properties as functions of the alloying element contents allowed us to conclude that the UTS level $ 1100 MPa at $K _ { C U } \geq 5 0 ~ \mathrm { J } / \mathrm { c m } ^ { 2 }$ can be attained for melts with carbon content of 0.84 to 0.99% at the concentrations of other elements of 8.8 to 9.7% for aluminum and 28.5 to 30.5% for manganese.
